book_summary: Numbers has chapters 1-16 complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. The wilderness narrative now includes scouting and fear-amplified reporting, communal panic and intercession, forty-year judgment and failed invasion, future land offerings and equal law for the sojourner, forgiveness for inadvertent error, the severe Sabbath wood-gatherer case, tassels as embodied remembrance, and the Korah-Dathan-Abiram conflict with its priestly-boundary claim, moral protest, collective-punishment crisis, and Aaron's life-preserving intercession. Strong anti-colonial, anti-racist, anti-militarization, anti-authoritarian, anti-clerical-abuse, anti-trauma, anti-xenophobia, anti-legalism, and anti-violence safeguards remain explicit.

chapters.notes: Twelve scouts, land and produce report, fortified cities, Caleb's minority report, Anakim/Nephilim fear rhetoric, anti-colonial safeguards.
Communal panic, return-to-Egypt proposal, Joshua and Caleb, Moses' intercession, pardon and wilderness judgment, scout deaths, and failed presumptuous invasion.
Future land offerings, sojourner-native equal law, first dough, inadvertent and raised-hand violations, severe Sabbath case, and tassels with blue cord.
Korah's coalition, communal-holiness claim, Dathan and Abiram's accusation, censer test, earth/fire judgments, hammered altar covering, plague, and Aaron between dead and living.

translation_notes: Numbers 13-14 belong to an ancient conquest narrative and must not justify modern colonization, ethnic cleansing, land seizure, displacement, or racial dehumanization.
The scouts' concrete observations must be distinguished from fear-amplified interpretation; 'we were grasshoppers' is self-perception and projection, not objective worth.
The people's panic in Numbers 14 is trauma-laden fear for families and must not be mocked or used to condemn anxious people.
Moses' intercession is legitimate covenant argument, and Numbers 14:20 explicitly speaks pardon even while severe consequences remain.
The failed invasion shows that remorse does not authorize violence and religious urgency cannot replace Divine leading.
Numbers 15 repeatedly gives one statute and one instruction to native and sojourner.
Inadvertent error receives covering and forgiveness; deliberate raised-hand defiance is treated differently.
The Sabbath wood-gatherer execution is source-preserved severe ancient law and categorically blocked from modern reenactment or vigilantism.
Tassels and the blue cord are embodied memory aids, not superiority markers or grounds for clothing enforcement.
Korah's claim that the whole congregation is holy echoes a genuine Torah principle; the narrative dispute concerns priestly access and power and must not make leaders immune from criticism.
Dathan and Abiram's accusation that Moses failed to deliver promised abundance is preserved as serious political protest, even within the rebellion narrative.
Moses and Aaron intercede against collective destruction, asking whether all should suffer for one person's wrong.
Household and child deaths in Numbers 16 require explicit collective-punishment critique; Numbers 26:11 later says Korah's sons did not die.
Aaron's final movement—running into danger and standing between dead and living—is the chapter's clearest model of life-preserving authority.
Mystical companion layer remains interpretive and does not replace source-audited restored text.
source_witness_audit_flags: Numbers 13 scout names, route, geography, and fruit report require textual and archaeological-context audit without weaponizing archaeology for modern land claims.
Numbers 13 Anakim/Nephilim terminology requires lexical, mythic, and reception-history audit.
Numbers 14 pardon-versus-consequence tension requires theological and trauma-informed audit.
Numbers 14 intergenerational wilderness consequences require anti-inherited-guilt safeguards.
Numbers 14 failed invasion should be crosslinked with Deuteronomy 1.
Numbers 15 offering measures require arithmetic and comparison with Leviticus/Numbers festival offerings.
Numbers 15 one-law language requires sojourner inclusion and historical-boundary audit.
Numbers 15:30 raised-hand terminology requires lexical/legal audit.
Numbers 15 Sabbath execution requires ancient-law and categorical anti-violence reception safeguards.
Numbers 15 tassels/tekhelet require textile, ritual, and later Jewish reception audit.
Numbers 16 Korah coalition composition and chronology require source/textual audit.
Numbers 16 all-congregation-holy claim requires anti-authoritarian and anti-clerical-abuse framing.
Numbers 16 Dathan-Abiram counter-narrative requires political and liberation-rhetoric audit.
Numbers 16 household deaths require collective-punishment critique and crosslink with Numbers 26:11.
Numbers 16 censer verses cross chapter-number differently in Hebrew and many English traditions and require versification audit.
Numbers 16 plague language requires anti-stigma and anti-victim-blaming safeguards.
next_actions: Continue in a larger batch with Numbers 17-21: Aaron's budding staff, priestly and Levite duties/support, red-heifer purification, Miriam's death and waters of Meribah, Edom's refusal, Aaron's death, Arad conflict, bronze serpent, travel stations, and Sihon/Og battles, with anti-authoritarian, anti-colonial, anti-stigma, grief, and anti-violence safeguards.
